agent_output: {"integration_assessment":{"component_interactions":[{"from":"SQS Queue","to":"Image Worker","status":"broken","issue":"Catastrophic throughput mismatch: 300 jobs/min inbound vs 15-20 jobs/min processing capacity. Queue will grow unboundedly (~280 msgs/min backlog). Messages will sit in queue far beyond visibility timeout, causing reprocessing storms and duplicate work."},{"from":"Image Worker","to":"SQS (message deletion)","status":"risk","issue":"With batch of 10 messages at ~3s each, a full batch takes ~30s to process — exactly the visibility timeout. Messages at the end of the batch will become visible again before deletion, causing duplicate processing. This is a near-certainty under normal load, not an edge case."},{"from":"Image Worker","to":"SQS (failed messages)","status":"broken","issue":"No dead-letter queue configured. Poison messages (corrupt images, unsupported formats, oversized files) will retry infinitely, consuming processing capacity from an already starved worker and never alerting anyone."}],"contract_compliance":"SQS consumption contract is fundamentally broken: visibility timeout is too short for batch processing time, and there is no DLQ redrive policy to bound retries.","data_flow_integrity":"Duplicate processing is near-guaranteed under load. Any job with side effects (writing to S3, updating a database, sending notifications) will produce duplicate outputs. No idempotency mechanism mentioned."},"observability_review":{"logging":{"coverage":"gaps","gaps":["No queue depth / backlog logging — operator cannot see the system is drowning","No per-job duration logging to detect slow jobs approaching visibility timeout","No duplicate detection logging — same message processed twice will look like two normal jobs","No memory/CPU resource utilization logging for the worker process"]},"metrics":{"coverage":"Minimal — two counters are necessary but nowhere near sufficient","gaps":["queue_depth / approximate_number_of_messages — the single most important metric for this system","job_duration_seconds (histogram) — needed to detect drift toward visibility timeout","messages_in_flight — to detect reprocessing storms","queue_age_of_oldest_message — to detect growing backlog","duplicate_jobs_detected — if idempotency is added","worker_utilization — ratio of busy time to wall clock time","failed_jobs broken down by error type (transient vs permanent)"]},"tracing":{"coverage":"No distributed tracing mentioned","gaps":["No trace/correlation ID from SQS message through processing pipeline","Cannot trace a single image from enqueue to completion across services","No way to correlate upstream request with worker job"]},"debugging_capability":"Poor. At 3am, an on-call engineer would see jobs_processed_total flatline and jobs_failed_total spike but have no way to determine: is the queue backed up? Are messages being reprocessed? Is a poison message starving the queue? Are we OOM? The two counters tell you something is wrong but nothing about why."},"resilience_assessment":{"failure_modes":[{"scenario":"Peak load (300 jobs/min sustained)","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"CRITICAL: Add autoscaling. At minimum, scale workers to ceil(300/17) ≈ 18 instances to match peak throughput. Use SQS ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible as the scaling metric."},{"scenario":"Poison message (unprocessable image)","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"CRITICAL: Configure a dead-letter queue with maxReceiveCount=3. Add alerting on DLQ depth. Process/inspect DLQ messages separately."},{"scenario":"Duplicate processing from visibility timeout expiry","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"CRITICAL: Either (a) extend visibility timeout to at least 120s to cover worst-case batch time with safety margin, or (b) process messages individually and call ChangeMessageVisibility to extend per-message, or (c) implement idempotency keys. Ideally do both (a) and (c)."},{"scenario":"Worker crash mid-batch","handling":"partial","recommendation":"Messages will become visible after 30s timeout — this is the one scenario where current config accidentally works. But without a DLQ, any message that caused the crash will crash the worker again on retry."},{"scenario":"Downstream dependency failure (e.g., S3 for output)","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"No circuit breaker or backoff mentioned. Worker will burn through retries on every message, all failing, consuming visibility timeout windows. Add circuit breaker to stop pulling from SQS when downstream is unhealthy."},{"scenario":"Memory exhaustion from large images","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"No mention of image size limits or memory guards. A batch of 10 large images could OOM the single worker process. Add per-image size validation and memory limits."}],"retry_config":"Unbounded implicit retries via SQS re-delivery. No explicit retry logic, no maxReceiveCount, no exponential backoff. Poison messages retry forever.","timeout_config":"Visibility timeout of 30s is dangerously close to batch processing time (~30s for 10 messages). Should be at least 3-5x the expected processing time per batch. Recommend 120-180s.","circuit_breakers":"None. Worker will continue pulling and failing if downstream services are unavailable.","graceful_degradation":"None. No backpressure mechanism — worker pulls at max rate regardless of its own health or downstream availability."},"operational_readiness":{"deployment_ready":false,"rollback_capable":false,"rollback_plan":"No rollback plan documented. Since the worker is a queue consumer, rollback means deploying the previous version — but with no autoscaling and a single instance, any deployment causes a processing gap that adds to the already-unmanageable backlog.","monitoring_coverage":"Insufficient. Two counters (processed/failed totals) without queue depth, processing latency, duplicate detection, or resource utilization metrics. Cannot detect the primary failure mode (backlog growth) with current metrics.","on_call_documentation":"No runbook mentioned. On-call engineer has no documentation for: how to drain the queue, how to identify poison messages, how to manually scale, what downstream dependencies exist, or what 'healthy' looks like for this service.","new_infrastructure_required":{"new_env_vars":[],"new_services":["Autoscaling group or ECS service with scaling policy","Dead-letter queue for failed messages"],"new_queues_topics":["SQS dead-letter queue with redrive policy (maxReceiveCount=3)"],"config_changes":["Increase SQS visibility timeout from 30s to 120-180s","Configure SQS redrive policy pointing to DLQ","Configure autoscaling policy based on ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible"],"infra_provisioning":["Autoscaling infrastructure (ASG, ECS Service, or equivalent) — minimum 15-20 worker instances for peak load","CloudWatch alarms for queue depth, DLQ depth, and worker error rates"]},"database_safety":{"has_migrations":false,"reversible":false,"data_preserving":false,"large_table_impact":false,"details":"No database migrations mentioned. Primary data safety concern is duplicate image processing producing duplicate outputs in whatever storage system receives results."},"verdict":"NOT READY"},"escalations":[{"type":"infrastructure_gap","issue":"15-20x throughput deficit at peak load with no autoscaling","reason":"Worker processes 15-20 jobs/min against 300 jobs/min inbound. Queue backlog grows at ~280 messages/min. Within 1 hour of peak load, backlog exceeds 16,000 messages. This is not a scaling concern — it is a fundamental capacity failure. The system cannot perform its primary function at stated load requirements.","routed_to":"Coach K"},{"type":"infrastructure_gap","issue":"No dead-letter queue — poison messages cause infinite retry loops","reason":"Without a DLQ and maxReceiveCount, any unprocessable message (corrupt image, unsupported format) retries forever. This consumes processing capacity from an already critically under-provisioned worker and generates unbounded cost via SQS API calls. This is a standard SQS operational requirement, not an optimization.","routed_to":"Coach K"},{"type":"infrastructure_gap","issue":"Visibility timeout nearly guarantees duplicate processing","reason":"Batch of 10 messages × ~3s each = ~30s, which equals the visibility timeout. End-of-batch messages will regularly become visible before deletion, causing duplicates. With no idempotency mechanism, this means duplicate side effects (duplicate S3 writes, duplicate database records, duplicate notifications). Must extend visibility timeout and implement idempotency.","routed_to":"Coach K"},{"type":"missing_observability","issue":"Cannot detect primary failure mode (queue backlog growth) with current metrics","reason":"The two most important operational signals for an SQS worker — queue depth and message age — are not tracked. The system could be drowning with a 50,000 message backlog and the only symptom visible in current metrics would be jobs_processed_total incrementing normally. An on-call engineer would have no idea anything is wrong until users report images not being processed.","routed_to":"Coach K"}],"confidence":{"level":95,"high_confidence_areas":["Throughput math is straightforward: 300 inbound vs 15-20 processed = system cannot keep up","Visibility timeout vs batch processing time collision is arithmetic, not speculation","Missing DLQ is a stated fact with clear operational consequences"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact processing time per image (stated as 2-4s, actual may vary)","Whether downstream writes are idempotent (not specified, assumed not)","Whether there is any external autoscaling not mentioned in the spec"],"assumptions":["Processing is truly single-threaded as stated","The 300 jobs/min peak load figure is accurate","No external autoscaling or load management exists outside the described system","Image processing has side effects that would be harmful if duplicated"]}}

Messages will sit in queue far beyond visibility timeout, causing reprocessing storms and duplicate work."},{"from":"Image Worker","to":"SQS (message deletion)","status":"risk","issue":"With batch of 10 messages at ~3s each, a full

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this image processing worker for production readiness.

Key issues:
1. Capacity: 300 jobs/min incoming, 15-20 jobs/min processing = massive queue backlog, messages will exceed visibility timeout
2. No DLQ = poison messages retry forever
3. No autoscaling
4. Visibility timeout too short for batch processing
5. Health endpoint is shallow
6. Missing metrics for queue depth, processing latency, etc.
